title: The Challenges of Governing Post-Brexit Britain
source: The Guardian - The Economist (Web Edition) 16 May 2026
content:
In post-Brexit Britain, exhausted centrists seem incapable of bringing about meaningful change or defeating populist challenges from both the left and right. The nation has cycled through five heads of government in just six years, replacing prime ministers at an astonishing rate. Despite this instability, the country remains governable, and its institutions still function effectively. Prime Minister Sir Keir Starmer often blames his difficulties on external factors, but many of his problems stem from a fundamental lack of political character and vision. Defending a struggling leader merely out of fear of something worse only fuels the populist insurgency that centrists desperately want to avoid.

It is true that the prime minister has faced significant headwinds since taking office. Real wages in Britain have barely grown over the last twenty years, and the country's departure from the European Union has noticeably lowered its GDP per person. Successive governments, terrified of angering local opposition, have failed to address a chronic lack of productivity growth. As a result of Brexit, Liz Truss’s brief premiership, and soaring energy prices, Britain’s government bonds now have the highest yields in the G7. Unfortunately, Starmer has compounded these economic woes with poor political maneuvering and indecision.

Perversely, Labour’s massive 165-seat parliamentary majority has turned out to be a source of internal instability. In a first-past-the-post electoral system, small changes in the share of votes can lead to wild swings in the number of seats a party expects to win. As Labour’s popularity has ebbed, many of its anxious Members of Parliament have been tempted to rebel to save their jobs. Despite this unrest, Starmer originally had a strong mandate to govern effectively and enact meaningful reforms. Opportunities for growth existed in improving relations with Europe, cutting planning red tape, and curbing unsustainable welfare programs.

A large part of the reason Britain has not thrived like other nations facing similar headwinds is Starmer himself. Even before taking power, he restricted his government with manifesto commitments not to raise key taxes, limiting his fiscal flexibility. His proposed reforms were often painful enough to alarm voters but too small to have a meaningful positive effect on the broader economy. The boldest initiatives never materialized; there was no major tax reset, brave welfare reform, or ambitious rapprochement with the EU. When he did attempt to speed up planning processes, he wavered and retreated as soon as he encountered political resistance.

As prime minister, Starmer lacks the necessary authority and clarity to articulate a unifying vision for the country. Modern policymaking requires voters to trust that their leader has the instincts to weigh evidence and make sensible, forward-looking decisions. Recent polling indicates that only a small fraction of the electorate wants him to remain in office, leaving Labour MPs in a state of panic. Ultimately, whoever leads the country must possess the political skill to chart a clear path for growth and fiercely defend it. Without strong centrist leadership, the nation risks succumbing to further division and the rising influence of political extremists.
